highlights: Terrace of the Leper King is located in Angkor Thom, in a large gateway to the north of the Royal Terrace. The Terrace At Angkor Thom there is a long terraces in front of the Royal Palace domain, named the Elephants’ Terrace. Close to its northern section stands the Terrace of the leper King. They both have an internal trench-like structure carved with mythological figures. It is not clear if the galleries were purposely buried to be hidden from view or created by a process of progressive enlargement of the building requiring new external walls. There was also the perpetual twittering of elusive birds, spider webs stretching out in tubular traps and, scattered here and there, blocks of sandstone and reddish laterite. Since 2016, a team of archaeologists led by Brice Vincent – an associate professor at the French School of Asian Studies (EFEO) and head of the EFEO's study center in Siem Reap, the neighboring city – has been drilling windows into the past in this small area behind the Terrace of the Leper King. The terrace of the Leper King is a monolithic cylindrical structure with a diameter of 25 m and a wall height of 6 m. It has two galleries with bas-reliefs: an external and an internal one. The inner gallery, unlike the outer one, is hidden from view and is located a couple of meters behind the outer wall, the entrance to it is located from the south,  ...
